821	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
822	
823	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
824	reduces, via the locality criterion
825	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
826	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
827	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
828	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
829	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
830	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
831	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
832	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
833	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
834	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
835	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
836	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
837	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
838	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
839	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
840	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
841	
842	\begin{lemma}
843	
844	\leanok
845	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
846	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
847	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
848	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
849	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
850	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
851	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
852	  % L920--926).
853	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
854	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
855	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
856	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
857	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
858	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
859	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
860	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of the
861	  base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible
862	  with restriction to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
863	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
864	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
865	  by the affine-open locality criterion
866	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
867	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
868	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
869	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
870	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
871	
872	\end{lemma}
873	
874	\begin{proof}
875	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
876	  By the affine-open locality criterion
877	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the base-change map is an isomorphism
878	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules as soon as its restriction to the sections over each
879	  affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
880	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
881	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
882	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
883	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
884	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
885	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
886	
887	  \medskip
888	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
889	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, the base-change map is the image,
890	  under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
891	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
892	  \[
893	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
894	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
895	  \]
896	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
897	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
898	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
899	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
900	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
901	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
902	
903	  \medskip
904	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
905	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
906	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
907	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
908	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
909	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
910	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
911	  \begin{itemize}
912	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
913	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
914	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
915	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
916	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
917	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
918	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
919	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
920	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
921	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
922	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
923	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
924	  \end{itemize}
925	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
926	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
927	  restricted counterpart — shows that the restriction of the base-change map to
928	  sections over \(U\) is itself the base-change map formed from the cartesian square
929	  restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
930	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
931	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
932	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
933	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
934	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
935	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
936	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
937	  obligation beyond them.
938	
939	  \medskip
940	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
941	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
942	  that the base-change map restricts to an isomorphism on sections over each affine
943	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2, precisely the
944	  affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with \emph{no} flatness
945	  hypothesis, by the section-level computation
946	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
947	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
948	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion
949	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} to this family of per-open
950	  isomorphisms shows that the base-change map is an isomorphism, as asserted.
951	\end{proof}
952	
953	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
954	
955	The section-level identification
956	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
957	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close, and we prove it \emph{directly on
958	global sections}, with no abstract adjoint-mate tower. In the affine--affine model the
959	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
960	proved tilde dictionaries, exactly the \(R'\)-base change of the algebraic unit of
961	extension of scalars; post-composing with the canonical regrouping isomorphism
962	\((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) identifies it with the canonical
963	base-change comparison, an isomorphism carrying no flatness hypothesis. The computation
964	factors through three named reads — the domain read, the codomain read, and the single
965	\emph{section-level identity} that the conjugated map is the base change of the unit —
966	together with the one Mathlib-absent crux identifying the pullback cone legs with the
967	\(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps of the tensor inclusions
968	(Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}). Two Mathlib results are recorded
969	first as dependency anchors.
970	
971	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of a pair of affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps]
972	  \label{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}
973	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}
974	  \mathlibok

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
352	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
354	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean. As of iter-020 the present theorem IS a closed, axiom-clean Lean
408	  %   declaration (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational); the ambient subquotient route
409	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
410	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	
413	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
414	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
415	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
416	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
417	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
418	%  following short exact sequence
419	%  $$
420	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
421	%  $$
422	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
423	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
435	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
436	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
437	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
438	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
439	
440	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
441	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
442	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
443	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
444	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
445	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
446	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
447	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
448	  is the number on which we induct.
449	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
450	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
451	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
452	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
453	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
454	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
455	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
456	
457	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
458	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
459	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
460	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
461	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
462	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
463	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
464	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
465	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
466	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
467	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
468	
469	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
470	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
471	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
472	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
473	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
474	  \[
475	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
476	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
477	  \]
478	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
479	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
480	  with
481	  \[
482	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
483	    \qquad (n > N),
484	  \]
485	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
486	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
487	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
488	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
489	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
490	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
491	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
492	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
493	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
497	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
498	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
499	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
500	
501	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
502	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
503	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
504	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
505	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
506	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
507	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
508	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
509	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
510	
511	\begin{lemma}\leanok
512	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
513	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
514	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
516	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
517	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
518	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
519	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
520	  \(F\):
521	  \[
522	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
523	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
524	  \]
525	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
526	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
527	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
528	  the Cauchy product.
529	\end{lemma}
530	
531	\begin{lemma}\leanok
532	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
533	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
536	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
537	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
538	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
539	  \[
540	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
541	  \]
542	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
543	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
544	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
545	  \[
546	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
547	  \]
548	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
549	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
550	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
551	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
552	\end{lemma}
553	
554	\begin{proof}
555	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
556	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
557	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
558	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
559	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
560	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
561	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
562	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
563	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
564	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
565	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
566	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
567	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
568	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
569	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
570	\end{proof}
571	
572	\begin{definition}\leanok
573	[Rational Hilbert function]
574	  \label{def:ratHilb}
575	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
576	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
577	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
578	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
579	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
580	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
581	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
582	  \[
583	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
584	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
585	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
586	  \]
587	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
588	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
589	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
590	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
591	\end{definition}
592	
593	\begin{lemma}\leanok
594	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
595	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
596	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
597	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
598	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
599	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
600	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
601	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
602	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
603	\end{lemma}
604	
605	\begin{lemma}\leanok
606	[Raising the pole order]
607	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
608	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
609	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
610	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
611	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
612	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
613	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
614	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
615	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
616	\end{lemma}
617	
618	\begin{lemma}\leanok
619	[Closure under difference]
620	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
622	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
623	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
624	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
625	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
626	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
627	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
628	\end{lemma}
629	
630	\begin{lemma}\leanok
631	[Closure under right shift]
632	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
634	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
635	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
636	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
637	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
638	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
639	  Lean.
640	\end{lemma}

642	\begin{lemma}\leanok
643	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
644	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
645	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
646	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
647	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
648	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
649	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
650	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
651	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
652	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
653	\end{lemma}
654	
655	\begin{proof}
656	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
657	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
658	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
659	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
660	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
661	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
662	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
663	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
664	\end{proof}

666	\begin{lemma}\leanok
667	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
668	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
669	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
670	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
671	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
672	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
673	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
674	  \[
675	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
676	  \]
677	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
678	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
679	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
680	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
681	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
682	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
683	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
684	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
685	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
686	\end{lemma}
687	
688	\begin{proof}
689	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
690	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
691	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
692	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
693	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
694	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
695	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
696	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
697	\end{proof}

699	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
700	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
702	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
703	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
704	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
705	  % Polynomials" (read from
706	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
707	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
708	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
709	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
710	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
711	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
712	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
713	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
714	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
715	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
716	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
717	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
718	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
719	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
720	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
721	  \[
722	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
723	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
724	  \]
725	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
726	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
727	\end{theorem}
728	
729	\begin{proof}
730	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
731	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
732	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
733	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
734	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
735	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
736	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
737	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
738	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
739	  function
740	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
741	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
742	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
743	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
744	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
745	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
746	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
747	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
748	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
749	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
750	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
751	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
752	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
753	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
754	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
755	\end{proof}

757	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
758	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
759	
760	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
761	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
762	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
763	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
764	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
765	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
766	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
767	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
768	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
769	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
770	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
771	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
772	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
773	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
774	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
775	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
776	
777	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
778	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
779	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
780	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
781	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
782	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
783	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
784	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
785	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
786	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
787	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
788	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
789	
790	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
791	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
792	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
793	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
794	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
795	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
796	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
797	\(x \in R_1\).
798	
799	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}

801	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
802	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
803	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
804	  \mathlibok
805	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
806	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
807	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
808	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
809	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
810	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
811	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
812	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
813	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
814	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
815	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).

904	\end{lemma}

906	\begin{proof}
907	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
908	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
909	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
910	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
911	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
912	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
913	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
914	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
915	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
916	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
917	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
918	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
919	\end{proof}

921	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
922	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}

924	  \mathlibok

954	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (matches the landed decl verbatim).
955	  %   Finiteness is the `RingHom.Finite` of the composite ring map
956	  %   R[X_1,…,X_n] ↪ R[X_0,…,X_n] ↠ R[X_0,…,X_n]/(p)
957	  %   (constant inclusion X_j ↦ X_{j+1} = MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ, then
958	  %   Ideal.Quotient.mk). A caller discharges a `RingHom.Finite` goal, not `Module.Finite`.
959	  %     (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ)
960	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)
961	  %       (hp : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).leadingCoeff) :
962	  %       ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
963	  %           (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ).toRingHom :
964	  %             MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →+* (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})).Finite
965	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
966	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
967	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
968	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
969	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
970	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
971	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
972	\end{lemma}

974	\begin{proof}
975	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
976	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
977	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
978	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
979	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
980	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
981	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
982	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
983	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
984	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
985	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
986	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
987	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
988	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
989	\end{proof}

994	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
995	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
996	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
997	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
998	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
999	  % NOTE (shared engine): the single-variable Nagata change-of-variables together with
1000	  % the denominator-clearing after inverting one element of A is the SAME engine the
1001	  % Noether-normalisation step L4 (lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, via its sub-lemma
1002	  % lem:gf_clear_one_denominator) requires. It should be built once in Lean and reused by
1003	  % both: a general "make a nonzero F monic in the last variable and clear the leading
1004	  % coefficient into one Localization.Away g" helper discharges this lemma and feeds L4.
1005	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1006	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
1007	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1008	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1009	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1010	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
1011	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
1012	  %   Write A_g := Localization.Away g and T_g := LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T.
1013	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
1014	  %       (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
1015	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1016	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1017	  %       [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1018	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1019	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
1020	  %         (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1021	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
1022	  %         -- NOTE: the redundant canonical `Module A_g T_g` existential was
1023	  %         -- dropped from the landed decl (it is always `inferInstance`; bundling it created an
1024	  %         -- opaque fvar breaking OreLocalization instance matching at the L5 use-site).
1025	  %         (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
1026	  %                 (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1027	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
1028	  %         Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1029	  %           (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
1030	  %   one may always take m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); the strong induction
1031	  %   accepts any m' < d.
1032	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1033	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1034	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1035	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1036	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1037	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1038	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1039	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1040	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
1041	\end{lemma}
1042	
1043	\begin{proof}
1044	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1045	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1046	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1047	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1048	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1049	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1050	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1051	
1052	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1053	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1054	
1055	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1056	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1057	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1058	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1059	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1060	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1061	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1062	
1063	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1064	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1065	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1066	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1067	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1068	  \(A_g\).
1069	
1070	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1071	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1072	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1073	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1074	
1075	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1076	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1077	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1078	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1079	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1080	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1081	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1082	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1083	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1084	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1085	
1086	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1087	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1088	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1089	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1090	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1091	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1092	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1093	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1094	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1095	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1096	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1097	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1098	
1099	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1100	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1101	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1102	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1103	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1104	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1105	
1106	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1107	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1108	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1109	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1110	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1111	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1112	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1113	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1114	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1115	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1116	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1117	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1118	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1119	
1120	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1121	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1122	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1123	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1124	\end{proof}
