1126	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1127	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1128	
1129	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1130	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1131	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1132	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1133	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1134	
1135	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1136	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1140	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1141	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1142	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1143	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1144	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1145	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1146	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1147	\end{lemma}
1148	\begin{proof}
1149	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1150	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1151	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1152	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1153	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1154	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1155	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1159	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1160	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1161	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1162	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1163	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1164	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1165	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	\begin{proof}
1168	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1169	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1170	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1171	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1172	\end{proof}
1173	
1174	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1175	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1177	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1178	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1179	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1180	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1181	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1182	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1186	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1187	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1188	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1189	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1190	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1191	  these conditions gives the claim.
1192	\end{proof}
1193	
1194	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1195	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1196	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1197	  \mathlibok
1198	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1199	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1200	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1201	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1202	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1203	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1204	\end{lemma}
1205	
1206	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1207	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1208	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1209	  \mathlibok
1210	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1211	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1212	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1213	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1214	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1215	\end{lemma}
1216	
1217	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1218	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1220	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1221	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1222	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1223	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1224	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1225	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1226	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1227	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1228	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1229	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1230	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1231	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1232	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1233	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1234	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1235	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1236	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1237	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1238	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1239	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1240	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1241	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1242	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1243	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1244	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1245	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1246	\end{lemma}
1247	
1248	\begin{proof}
1249	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1250	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1251	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1252	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1253	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1254	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1255	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1256	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1257	
1258	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1259	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1260	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1261	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1262	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1263	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1264	
1265	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1266	  \[
1267	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1268	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1269	  \]
1270	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1271	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1272	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1273	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1274	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1275	
1276	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1277	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1278	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1279	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1280	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1281	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1282	\end{proof}
1283	
1284	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1285	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1287	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1288	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1289	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1290	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1291	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1292	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1293	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1294	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1295	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1296	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1297	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1298	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1299	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1300	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1301	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1302	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1303	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1304	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1305	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1306	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1307	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1308	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1309	  core of the induction).}
1310	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1311	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1312	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1313	
1314	\end{lemma}
1315	
1316	\begin{proof}
1317	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1318	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1319	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1320	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1322	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1323	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1324	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1325	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1326	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1327	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1328	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1329	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1330	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1331	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1332	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1333	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1334	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1335	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1336	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1337	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1338	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1339	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1340	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1341	
1342	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1343	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1344	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1345	
1346	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1347	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1348	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1349	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1350	  \begin{enumerate}
1351	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1352	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1353	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1354	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1355	      \(P_d\)-modules
1356	      \[
1357	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1358	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1359	      \]
1360	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1361	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1362	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1363	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1364	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1365	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1366	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1367	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1368	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1369	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1370	      to \(A_g\).
1371	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1372	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1373	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1374	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1375	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1376	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1377	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1378	      over \(A_f\).
1379	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1380	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1381	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1382	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1383	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1384	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1385	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1386	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1387	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1388	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1389	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1390	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1391	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1392	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1393	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1394	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1395	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1396	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1397	      completes the induction.
1398	  \end{enumerate}
1399	\end{proof}

1401	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1402	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1403	
1404	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1405	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1406	
1407	\begin{lemma}
1408	
1409	
1410	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1411	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1413	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1414	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1415	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1416	\end{lemma}
1417	\begin{proof}
1418	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1419	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1420	\end{proof}
1421	
1422	\begin{lemma}
1423	
1424	
1425	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1426	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1427	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1428	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1429	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1430	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1431	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1432	  is free over \(A_f\).
1433	\end{lemma}
1434	\begin{proof}
1435	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1436	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1437	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1438	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1439	\end{proof}
1440	
1441	
1442	\begin{theorem}
1443	
1444	
1445	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1446	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1448	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1449	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1450	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1451	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1452	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1453	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1454	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1455	  %
1456	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1457	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1458	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1459	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1460	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1461	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1462	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1463	  %
1464	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
1465	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1466	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
1467	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1468	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1469	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1470	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1471	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1472	
1473	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1474	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1475	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1476	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1477	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1478	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1479	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1480	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1481	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1482	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1483	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1484	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1485	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1486	
1487	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1488	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1489	\end{theorem}
1490	
1491	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1492	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1493	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1494	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1495	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1496	% by restricting to a non-empty
1497	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1498	\begin{proof}
1499	  
1500	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1501	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1502	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1503	
1504	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1505	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1506	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1507	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1508	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1509	
1510	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1511	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1512	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1513	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1514	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1515	
1516	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1517	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1518	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1519	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1520	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1521	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1522	
1523	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1524	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1525	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1526	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1527	\end{proof}

1529	\section{Mathlib status}
1530	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}

1242	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1243	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1244	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1245	% used in the codomain-read computation.

1247	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1248	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1249	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1251	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1252	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1253	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1254	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1255	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1256	\end{lemma}
1257	\begin{proof}
1258	
1259	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1260	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1261	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1262	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1263	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1264	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1265	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1266	\end{proof}
1267	
1268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1269	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1270	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1272	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1273	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1274	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1275	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1276	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	\begin{proof}
1279	
1280	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1281	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1282	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1283	\end{proof}

1326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1327	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1328	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1329	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1330	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1331	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1332	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1333	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1334	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1335	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1336	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1337	
1338	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1339	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1340	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1341	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1342	  dictionaries,
1343	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1344	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1345	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1346	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1347	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1348	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1349	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1350	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1351	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1352	  \[
1353	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1354	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1355	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1356	  \]
1357	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1358	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1359	
1360	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1361	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1362	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1363	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1364	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1365	\end{proof}

1457	\begin{lemma}[Unit-across-conjugate coherence]
1458	  \label{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
1459	  \lean{CategoryTheory.unit_conjugateEquiv}
1460	  \mathlibok
1461	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1462	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be two
1463	  adjunctions between the same pair of categories, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\), and let
1464	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\) be a natural transformation of the left adjoints. Write
1465	  \(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\) for the conjugation (mate) bijection,
1466	  which carries \(\alpha\) to a natural transformation \(R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\) of the right
1467	  adjoints. Then for every object \(c\),
1468	  \[
1469	    (\eta_1)_c \circ \bigl(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha\bigr)_{L_1 c}
1470	      \;=\; (\eta_2)_c \circ R_2(\alpha_c) ,
1471	  \]
1472	  the transposed form of the conjugation relating the two units across the mate of \(\alpha\).
1473	\end{lemma}

1475	\begin{lemma}[Unit of a composite adjunction]
1476	  \label{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib}
1477	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.comp_unit_app}
1478	  \mathlibok
1479	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1480	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : F \dashv G\) (with \(F : C \to D\)) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : H \dashv I\)
1481	  (with \(H : D \to E\)) be composable adjunctions, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\). Then the
1482	  unit of the composite adjunction
1483	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1 . \mathrm{adj}_2 : (H \circ F) \dashv (G \circ I)\) is, at every object \(X\),
1484	  \[
1485	    \bigl(\mathrm{adj}_1 . \mathrm{adj}_2\bigr)\text{-unit}_X
1486	      \;=\; (\eta_1)_X \circ G\bigl((\eta_2)_{F X}\bigr).
1487	  \]
1488	\end{lemma}

1490	\begin{lemma}[The pullback--pushforward conjugate sends the pullback coherence to the pushforward coherence]
1491	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv}
1493	  \mathlibok
1494	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1495	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) be composable morphisms of schemes, and write
1496	  \((h^{*} \dashv h_{*})\) for the pullback--pushforward adjunction on \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
1497	  attached to a morphism \(h\). Under the conjugation (mate) bijection between the composite
1498	  adjunction \((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*})\) and the adjunction
1499	  \(\bigl((f\circ g)^{*} \dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\), the inverse pullback-composition coherence
1500	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is carried to the pushforward-composition
1501	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{hom}}\):
1502	  \[
1503	    \operatorname{conj}\!\Bigl((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*}),\;
1504	      \bigl((f\circ g)^{*}\dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\Bigr)
1505	      \bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\bigr)

1510	\begin{lemma}
1511	
1512	\leanok
1513	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1514	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1516	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1517	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1518	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1519	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1520	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1521	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1522	  composition coherences. Then
1523	  \[
1524	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1525	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1526	    \;=\;
1527	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1528	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1529	  \]
1530	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1531	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1532	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1533	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1534	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1535	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1536	\end{lemma}
1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1539	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1540	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1541	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1542	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1543	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1544	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1545	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1546	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1547	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1548	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N
1549	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1550	  the stated equation.
1551	\end{proof}

974	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
975	
976	\begin{lemma}\leanok
977	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
978	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
979	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
980	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
981	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
982	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
983	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
984	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
985	  \(n\),
986	  \[
987	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
988	  \]
989	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
990	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
991	\end{lemma}
992	
993	\begin{proof}
994	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
995	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
996	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
997	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
998	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
999	\end{proof}
1000	
1001	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1002	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
1003	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1004	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
1005	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1006	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1007	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
1008	  \[
1009	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
1010	  \]
1011	  Together with the independence of
1012	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
1013	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
1014	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
1015	\end{lemma}
1016	
1017	\begin{proof}
1018	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1019	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
1020	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
1021	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
1022	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
1023	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
1024	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
1025	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
1026	\end{proof}

1035	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1036	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1037	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1038	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1039	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1040	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1041	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1042	  \begin{itemize}
1043	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1044	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1045	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1046	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1047	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1048	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1049	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1050	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1051	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1052	  \end{itemize}
1053	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1054	  Hilbert function} is
1055	  \[
1056	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1057	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1058	  \]
1059	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1060	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1061	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1062	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1063	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1064	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1065	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1066	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1067	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1068	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1069	\end{definition}
1070	
1071	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1072	
1073	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1074	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1075	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1076	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1077	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1078	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1079	
1080	\begin{definition}\leanok
1081	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1082	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1083	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1084	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1085	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1086	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1087	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1088	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1089	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1090	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1091	\end{definition}
1092	
1093	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1094	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1095	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1096	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1097	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1098	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1099	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1100	\end{lemma}
1101	
1102	\begin{proof}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1105	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{proof}
1107	
1108	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1109	[Degree-shift commutation]
1110	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1111	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1112	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1113	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1114	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1115	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1116	  \[
1117	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1118	  \]
1119	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1120	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1121	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1122	\end{lemma}
1123	
1124	\begin{proof}
1125	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1126	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1127	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1128	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1129	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1130	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1131	\end{proof}
1132	
1133	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1134	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1135	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1136	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1137	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1138	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1139	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1140	\end{lemma}
1141	
1142	\begin{proof}
1143	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1144	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1145	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1146	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1147	\end{proof}
1148	
1149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1150	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1151	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1153	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1154	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1155	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1156	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1157	\end{lemma}
1158	
1159	\begin{proof}
1160	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1161	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1162	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1163	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1164	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1165	  homogeneous.
1166	\end{proof}
1167	
1168	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1169	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1170	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1172	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1173	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1174	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1175	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1176	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1177	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1178	\end{lemma}
1179	
1180	\begin{proof}
1181	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1182	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1183	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1184	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1185	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1186	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1187	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1188	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1189	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1190	  homogeneous.
1191	\end{proof}
1192	
1193	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1194	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1195	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1196	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1197	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1198	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1199	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1200	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1201	  this is recorded here.
1202	\end{lemma}
1203	
1204	\begin{proof}
1205	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1206	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1207	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1208	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1209	\end{proof}
1210	
1211	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1212	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1213	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1214	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1215	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1216	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1217	\end{lemma}
1218	
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1221	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1222	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1223	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1224	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1225	\end{proof}
1226	
1227	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1228	[Ambient image identity]
1229	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1230	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1231	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1232	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1233	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1234	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1235	  \[
1236	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1237	  \]
1238	\end{lemma}
1239	
1240	\begin{proof}
1241	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1242	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1243	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1244	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1245	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1246	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1247	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1248	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1249	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1250	\end{proof}
1251	
1252	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1253	[Ambient distributive law]
1254	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1255	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1256	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1257	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1258	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1259	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1260	  \[
1261	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1262	  \]
1263	\end{lemma}
1264	
1265	\begin{proof}
1266	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1267	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1268	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1269	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1270	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1271	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1272	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1273	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1274	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1275	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1276	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1277	\end{proof}
1278	
1279	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1280	
1281	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1282	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1283	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1284	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1285	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1286	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1287	
1288	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1289	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1290	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1292	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1293	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1294	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1295	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1296	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1297	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1298	\end{lemma}
1299	
1300	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1301	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1302	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1304	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1305	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1306	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1307	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1308	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1309	\end{lemma}
1310	
1311	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1312	[The kernel pair nests]
1313	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1314	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1315	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1316	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1317	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1318	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1319	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1320	\end{lemma}
1321	
1322	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1323	[The cokernel pair nests]
1324	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1325	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1326	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1327	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1328	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1329	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1330	\end{lemma}
1331	
1332	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1333	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1334	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1335	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1336	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1337	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1338	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1339	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1340	\end{lemma}
1341	
1342	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1343	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1344	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1346	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1347	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1348	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1349	\end{lemma}
1350	
1351	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1352	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1353	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1355	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1356	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1357	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1358	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1359	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1360	\end{lemma}
1361	
1362	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1363	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1364	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1366	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1367	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1368	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1369	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1370	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1371	\end{lemma}
1372	
1373	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1374	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1375	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1376	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1377	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1378	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1379	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1380	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1381	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1382	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1383	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1384	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1385	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1386	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1387	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1388	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1389	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1390	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1391	  \[
1392	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1393	    \qquad
1394	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1395	  \]
1396	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1397	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1398	  \begin{enumerate}
1399	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1400	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1401	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1402	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1403	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1404	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1405	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1406	  \end{enumerate}
1407	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1408	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1409	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1410	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1411	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1412	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1413	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1414	\end{lemma}
1415	
1416	\begin{proof}
1417	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1418	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1419	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1420	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1421	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1422	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1423	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1424	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1425	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1426	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1427	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1428	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1429	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1430	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1431	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1432	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1433	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1434	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1435	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1436	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1437	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1438	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1439	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1440	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1441	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1442	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1443	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1444	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1445	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1446	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1447	
1448	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1449	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1450	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1451	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1452	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1453	
1454	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1455	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1456	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1457	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1458	
1459	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1460	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1461	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1462	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1463	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1464	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1465	\end{proof}
1466	
1467	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1468	
1469	\begin{lemma}
1470	[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
1471	  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
1472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
1473	  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
1474	  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of
1475	  the ambient intersection: \(\dim_\kappa(\iota_p^{-1} S) = \dim_\kappa(p \cap S)\),
1476	  where \(\iota_p : p \to M\) is the canonical injection. Project-local helper consumed
1477	  by the two kernel-dimension computations of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}.
1478	\end{lemma}
1479	\begin{proof}
1480	  The inclusion \(\iota_p\) is injective, so it restricts to a \(\kappa\)-linear
1481	  isomorphism from \(\iota_p^{-1} S\) onto its image \(\iota_p(\iota_p^{-1} S) = p \cap S\)
1482	  (image of a comap under an injective map). An isomorphism preserves dimension, giving
1483	  the claimed equality.
1484	\end{proof}
1485	
1486	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1487	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1488	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1490	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1491	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1492	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1493	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1494	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1495	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
1496	  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
1497	  %   intersection dimension).
1498	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1499	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1500	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1501	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1502	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1503	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1504	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1505	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1506	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1507	  \[
1508	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1509	  \]
1510	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1511	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1512	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1513	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1514	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1515	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1516	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1517	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1518	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1519	\end{lemma}
1520	
1521	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1522	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1523	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1524	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1525	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1526	%  following short exact sequence
1527	%  $$
1528	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1529	%  $$
1530	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1531	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1532	\begin{proof}
1533	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1534	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1535	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1536	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1537	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1538	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1539	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1540	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1541	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1543	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1544	
1545	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1546	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1547	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1548	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1549	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1550	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1551	
1552	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1553	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1554	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1555	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1556	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1557	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1558	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1559	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1560	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1561	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1562	
1563	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1564	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1565	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1566	  \[
1567	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1568	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1569	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1570	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1571	  \]
1572	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1573	\end{proof}
1574	
1575	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1576	
1577	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1578	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1579	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1580	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1581	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1582	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1583	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1584	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1585	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1586	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1587	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1588	
1589	\begin{definition}\leanok
1590	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1591	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1592	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1593	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1594	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1595	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1596	  ring homomorphism}
1597	  \[
1598	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1599	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1600	  \]
1601	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1602	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1603	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1604	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1605	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1606	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1607	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1608	\end{definition}
1609	
1610	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1611	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1612	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1613	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1614	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1615	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1616	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1617	  \(t_i\).
1618	\end{lemma}
1619	
1620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1621	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1622	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1624	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1625	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1626	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1627	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1628	\end{lemma}
1629	
1630	\begin{definition}\leanok
1631	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1632	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1634	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1635	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1636	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1637	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1638	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1639	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1640	  is measured.
1641	\end{definition}
1642	
1643	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1644	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1645	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1647	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1648	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1649	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1650	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1651	\end{lemma}
1652	
1653	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1654	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1655	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1657	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1658	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1659	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1660	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1661	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1662	\end{lemma}
1663	
1664	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1665	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1666	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1667	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1668	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1669	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1670	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1671	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1672	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1673	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1674	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1675	\end{lemma}
1676	
1677	\begin{definition}\leanok
1678	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1679	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1680	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1681	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1682	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1683	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1684	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1685	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1686	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1687	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1688	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1689	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1690	\end{definition}
1691	
1692	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1693	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1694	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1695	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1696	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1697	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1698	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1699	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1700	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1701	\end{lemma}
