1867	\begin{proof}
1868	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1869	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1870	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1871	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1872	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1873	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1874	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1875	  \[
1876	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1877	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1878	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1879	  \]
1880	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1881	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1882	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1883	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1884	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1885	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1886	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1887	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1888	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1889	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1890	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1891	  \[
1892	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1893	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1894	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1895	  \]
1896	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1897	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1898	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1899	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1900	
1901	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1902	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1903	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1904	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1905	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1906	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1907	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1908	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1909	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1910	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1911	\end{proof}

1913	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1914	
1915	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1916	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1917	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1918	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1919	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1920	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1921	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1922	
1923	\begin{lemma}
1924	
1925	\leanok
1926	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1927	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1928	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1929	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1930	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1931	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1932	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1933	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1934	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1935	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1936	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1937	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1938	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1939	\end{lemma}
1940	
1941	\begin{lemma}
1942	
1943	\leanok
1944	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1945	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1946	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1947	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1948	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1949	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1950	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1951	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1952	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1953	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1954	\end{lemma}

1956	\begin{definition}
1957	
1958	\leanok
1959	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1960	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1961	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1962	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1963	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1964	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1965	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1966	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1967	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1968	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1969	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1970	  \[
1971	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
1972	  \]
1973	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
1974	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
1975	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
1976	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
1977	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
1978	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
1979	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
1980	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
1981	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
1982	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
1983	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
1984	  length-\(r\) datum.
1985	\end{definition}

1987	\begin{definition}
1988	
1989	\leanok
1990	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1991	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
1992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
1993	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1994	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
1995	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
1996	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1997	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1998	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
1999	  length-\(r\) datum
2000	  \[
2001	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2002	  \]
2003	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2004	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2005	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2006	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2007	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2008	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2009	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
2010	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
2011	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
2012	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2013	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2014	\end{definition}
2015	
2016	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2017	
2018	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2019	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2020	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2021	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2022	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2023	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2024	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2025	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2026	  \[
2027	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2028	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2029	  \]
2030	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2031	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2032	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2033	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2034	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2035	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2036	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2037	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2038	  \[
2039	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2040	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2041	  \]
2042	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2043	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2044	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2045	\end{lemma}
2046	
2047	\begin{proof}
2048	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2049	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2050	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2051	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2052	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2053	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2054	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2055	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2056	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2057	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2058	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2059	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2060	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2061	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2062	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2063	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2064	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2065	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2066	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2067	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2068	\end{proof}

2070	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2071	
2072	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2073	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2074	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2075	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2076	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2077	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2078	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2079	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2080	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2081	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2082	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2083	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2084	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2085	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2086	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2087	\end{lemma}
2088	
2089	\begin{lemma}
2090	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2091	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2092	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2093	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2094	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2095	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2096	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2097	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2098	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2099	\end{lemma}
2100	\begin{proof}
2101	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2102	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2103	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2104	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2105	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2106	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2107	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2108	\end{proof}
2109	
2110	\begin{lemma}
2111	
2112	\leanok
2113	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2114	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2116	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2117	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2118	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2119	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2120	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2121	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2122	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2123	  % L13908-L13910).
2124	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2125	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2126	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2127	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2128	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2129	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2130	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2131	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2132	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2133	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2134	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2135	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2136	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2137	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2138	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2139	\end{lemma}
2140	
2141	\begin{proof}
2142	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2143	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2144	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2145	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2146	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2147	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2148	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2149	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2150	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2151	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2152	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2153	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2154	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2155	
2156	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2157	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2158	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2159	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2160	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2161	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2162	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2163	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2164	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2165	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2166	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2167	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2168	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2169	  = 0\); independence follows.
2170	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2171	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2172	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2173	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2174	  %   is ever introduced.
2175	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2176	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2177	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2178	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2179	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2180	
2181	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2182	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2183	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2184	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2185	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2186	
2187	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2188	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2189	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2190	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2191	  and
2192	  \[
2193	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2194	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2195	  \]
2196	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2197	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2198	\end{proof}

2201	
2202	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2203	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2204	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2206	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2207	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2208	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2209	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2210	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2211	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2212	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2213	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2214	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2215	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2216	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2217	  degree-one generators).}
2218	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2219	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2220	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2221	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2222	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2223	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2224	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2225	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2226	  \(M\).
2227	\end{lemma}
2228	
2229	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2230	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2231	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2232	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2233	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2234	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2235	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2236	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2237	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2238	\begin{proof}
2239	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2240	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2241	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2243	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2244	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2245	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2246	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2247	
2248	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2249	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2250	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2251	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2252	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2253	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2254	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2255	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2256	
2257	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2258	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2259	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2260	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2261	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2262	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2263	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2264	  induction hypothesis applied
2265	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2266	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2267	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2268	  \[
2269	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2270	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2271	  \]
2272	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2273	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2274	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2275	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2276	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2277	\end{proof}

2279	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2280	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2281	
2282	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2283	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2284	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2285	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2286	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2287	
2288	\begin{definition}
2289	
2290	\leanok
2291	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2292	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2294	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2295	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2296	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2297	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2298	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2299	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2300	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2301	  % Polynomials" (read from
2302	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2303	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2304	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2305	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2306	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2307	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2308	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2310	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2311	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2312	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2313	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2314	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2315	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2316	  \[
2317	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2318	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2319	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2320	  \]
2321	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2322	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2323	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2324	
2325	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2326	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2327	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2328	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2329	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2330	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2331	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2332	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2333	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2334	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2335	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2336	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2337	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2338	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2339	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2340	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2341	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2342	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2343	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2344	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2345	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2346	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2347	
2348	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2349	\end{definition}
2350	
2351	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2352	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2353	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2355	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2356	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2357	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2358	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2359	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2360	  \(U\):
2361	  \[
2362	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2363	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2364	  \]
2365	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2366	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2367	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2368	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2369	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2370	\end{lemma}
2371	
2372	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2373	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2374	  localization]
2375	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2376	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2377	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2378	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2379	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2380	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2381	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2382	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2383	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2384	  \[
2385	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2386	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2387	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2388	  \]
2389	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2390	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2391	
2392	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2393	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2394	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2395	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2396	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2397	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2398	\end{lemma}
2399	
2400	\begin{proof}
2401	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2402	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2403	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2404	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2405	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2406	
2407	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2408	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2409	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2410	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2411	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2412	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2413	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2414	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2415	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2416	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2417	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2418	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2419	\end{proof}
2420	
2421	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2422	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2424	  \mathlibok
2425	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2426	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2427	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2428	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2429	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2430	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2431	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2432	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2433	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2434	\end{lemma}
2435	
2436	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2437	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2438	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
2439	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2440	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2441	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2442	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2443	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2444	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2445	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2446	  %  associated to $M$.
2447	  %  [...]
2448	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2449	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2450	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2451	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2452	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2453	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2454	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2455	  \begin{enumerate}
2456	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2457	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2458	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2459	    \item the section-restriction map
2460	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2461	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2462	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2463	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2464	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2465	  \end{enumerate}
2466	\end{lemma}
2467	
2468	\begin{proof}
2469	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2470	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2471	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2472	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2473	
2474	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2475	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2476	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2477	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2478	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2479	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2480	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2481	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2482	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2483	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2484	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2485	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2486	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2487	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2488	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2489	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2490	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2491	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2492	\end{proof}

2494	\begin{definition}
2495	
2496	\leanok
2497	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
2498	  \label{def:schematic_support}
2499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
2500	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
2501	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
2502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2503	  % Polynomials" (read from
2504	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2505	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2506	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2507	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2508	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2509	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2511	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2512	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
2513	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
2514	  \[
2515	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
2516	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
2517	  \]
2518	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
2519	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
2520	  \(X\).
2521	\end{definition}

2523	\begin{definition}\leanok
2524	[Schematic-support immersion]
2525	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
2526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
2527	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
2528	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2529	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2530	  \[
2531	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
2532	  \]
2533	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
2534	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
2535	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
2536	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
2537	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
2538	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
2539	\end{definition}

2541	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2542	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2544	  \mathlibok
2545	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2546	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2547	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2548	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2549	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2550	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2551	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2552	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2553	\end{lemma}
2554	
2555	\begin{definition}
2556	
2557	\leanok
2558	[Proper support over the base]
2559	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
2560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
2561	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
2562	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
2563	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
2564	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
2565	  % + L418-L419).
2566	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2567	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
2568	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
2569	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
2570	  %  [...]
2571	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2572	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
2573	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2574	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
2575	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
2576	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
2577	  \[
2578	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
2579	  \]
2580	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
2581	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
2582	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
2583	  functor's pullback action requires.

2612	\section{The Quot functor}
2613	\label{sec:quot_functor}
2614	
2615	\begin{definition}\leanok
2616	[Quot functor]
2617	  \label{def:quot_functor}
2618	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
2619	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
2620	
2621	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
2622	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
2623	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
2624	  % + L481-L498).
2625	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2626	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
2627	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
2628	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
2629	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
2630	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
2631	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
2632	  %
2633	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
2634	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
2635	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
2636	  %
2637	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
2638	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
2639	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
2640	  %
2641	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
2642	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
2643	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
2644	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2645	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
2646	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
2647	  %  well-defined, which gives
2648	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
2649	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
2650	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
2651	  %  [...]
2652	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
2653	  %  as a co-product
2654	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
2655	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
2656	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
2657	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
2658	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
2659	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
2660	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
2661	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2662	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2663	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
2664	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
2665	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
2666	  \[
2667	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
2668	  \]
2669	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
2670	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
2671	  \begin{enumerate}
2672	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
2673	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
2674	      which is flat over \(T\);
2675	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
2676	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
2677	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
2678	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
2679	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
2680	      \(\Phi\).
2681	  \end{enumerate}
2682	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
2683	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
2684	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
2685	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
2686	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
2687	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
2688	\end{definition}

2690	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
2691	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
2692	
2693	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
2694	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
2695	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
2696	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
2697	
2698	\begin{definition}\leanok
2699	[Grassmannian scheme]
2700	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
2701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
2702	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
2703	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
2704	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
2705	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
2706	  % + L773-L850).
2707	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2708	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
2709	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
2710	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
2711	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
2712	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
2713	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
2714	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
2715	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
2716	  %  contravariant functor
2717	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
2718	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
2719	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2720	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
2721	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
2722	  %  [...]
2723	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
2724	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
2725	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
2726	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
2727	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
2728	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
2729	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
2730	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
2731	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
2732	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
2733	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
2734	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2735	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
2736	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
2737	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
2738	  \[
2739	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
2740	    \qquad
2741	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
2742	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
2743	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
2744	  \]
2745	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
2746	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
2747	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
2748	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
2749	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
2750	\end{definition}

2819	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
2820	  Explained Ch.~5).}
2821	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
2822	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
2823	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
2824	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
2825	  equipped with a tautological quotient
2826	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
2827	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
2828	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
2829	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
2830	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.

2833	
2834	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
2835	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
2836	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
2837	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
2838	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
2839	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
2840	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
2841	\begin{proof}
2842	  
2843	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
2844	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
2845	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
2846	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
2847	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
2848	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
2849	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
2850	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
2851	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
2852	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
2853	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
2854	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
2855	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
2856	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
2857	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
2858	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
2859	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
2860	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
2861	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
2862	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
2863	
2864	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
2865	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
2866	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
2867	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
2868	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
2869	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
2870	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
2871	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
2872	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
2873	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
2874	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
2875	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
2876	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
2877	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
2878	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
2879	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
2880	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
2881	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
2882	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
2883	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
2884	\end{proof}

2886	\section{Out of scope}
2887	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
2888	
2889	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
2890	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
2891	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
2892	\begin{itemize}
2893	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
2894	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
2895	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
2896	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
2897	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
2898	    associated prerequisites -- not constructed in this chapter.
2899	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
2900	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
2901	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
2902	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
2903	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
2904	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
2905	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
2906	    Lean formalization is a prerequisite sub-task.
2907	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
2908	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
2909	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
2910	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
2911	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
2912	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
2913	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
2914	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
2915	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
2916	    subproject.
2917	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
2918	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
2919	    constructed in this chapter.
2920	\end{itemize}

1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}

1042	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1044	
1045	\begin{definition}
1046	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1047	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1049	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1050	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1051	  % affine patches" (read from
1052	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1053	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1054	  % "Therefore the schemes
1055	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1056	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1057	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1058	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1059	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1060	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1061	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1063	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1064	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1065	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1066	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1067	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1068	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1069	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1070	  \[
1071	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1072	  \]
1073	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1074	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1075	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1076	
1077	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1078	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1079	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1080	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1081	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1082	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1083	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1084	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1085	  follows.
1086	\end{definition}
1087	
1088	\section{Separatedness}
1089	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1090	
1091	\begin{lemma}
1092	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1093	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1094	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1095	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1096	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1097	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1098	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1099	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1100	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1101	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1102	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1104	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1105	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1106	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1107	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1108	\end{lemma}
1109	
1110	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1111	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1112	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1113	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1114	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1117	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1118	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1119	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1120	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1121	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1122	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1123	  homomorphism is surjective.
1124	
1125	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1126	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1127	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1128	  \[
1129	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1130	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1131	    \qquad
1132	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1133	  \]
1134	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1135	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1136	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1137	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1138	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1139	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1140	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1141	  and in particular
1142	  \[
1143	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1144	  \]
1145	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1146	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1147	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1148	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1149	  \[
1150	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1151	  \]
1152	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1153	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1154	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1155	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1156	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1157	\end{proof}
1158	
1159	\section{Properness}
1160	\label{sec:gr_proper}
1161	
1162	\begin{lemma}
1163	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1164	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
1165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
1166	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
1167	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from

1177	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that

1180	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
1181	  %
1182	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
1183	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1184	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
1185	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
1186	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
1187	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
1188	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
1189	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
1190	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
1191	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
1192	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1193	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
1194	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
1195	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1196	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1197	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1198	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
1199	\end{lemma}
1200	
1201	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
1202	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
1203	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1204	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
1205	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
1206	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
1207	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
1208	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
1209	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
1210	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1211	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
1212	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
1213	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
1214	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
1215	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
1216	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
1217	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1218	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
1219	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1220	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
1221	\begin{proof}
1222	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
1223	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
1224	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
1225	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1226	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1227	  \[
1228	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1229	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
1230	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
1231	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
1232	    \end{array}
1233	  \]
1234	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
1235	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
1236	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1237	
1238	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
1239	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
1240	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1241	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1242	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1243	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1244	  \(K\). Define
1245	  \[
1246	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1247	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1248	  \]
1249	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1250	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1251	  landing in \(U^J\).
1252	
1253	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1254	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1255	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1256	  \[
1257	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1258	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1259	    \qquad
1260	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1261	  \]
1262	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1263	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1264	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1265	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1266	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1267	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1268	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1269	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1270	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1271	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1272	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1273	
1274	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1275	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1276	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1277	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1278	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1279	  proper.
1280	\end{proof}
1281	
1282	\section{Out of scope}
1283	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1284	
1285	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1286	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1287	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1288	are not treated here:
1289	\begin{itemize}
1290	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1291	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1292	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1293	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1294	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1295	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1296	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1297	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1298	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1299	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1300	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1301	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1302	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1303	    that base change.
1304	\end{itemize}
1305

input.command:
cat .leandag/dag.json | python3 -c "
import json,sys
d=json.load(sys.stdin)
meta = d.get('meta', {})
print('META:', json.dumps(meta, indent=2))
nodes = d.get('nodes', [])
print(f'\\nTotal nodes: {len(nodes)}')
# Check for unknown_uses, isolated, unmatched
unknown = [n for n in nodes if n.get('unknown_uses')]
print(f'Nodes with unknown_uses: {len(unknown)}')
if unknown[:5]:
    for n in unknown[:5]:
        print('  ', n.get('label'), ':', n.get('unknown_uses'))
isolated = [n for n in nodes if not n.get('uses') and not n.get('used_by')]
print(f'Isolated nodes (no uses/used_by): {len(isolated)}')
if isolated[:5]:
    for n in isolated[:5]:
        print('  ', n.get('label'), ':', n.get('kind'))
"

subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
\end{proof}

\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}

\begin{lemma}
[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of
  the ambient intersection: \(\dim_\kappa(\iota_p^{-1} S) = \dim_\kappa(p \cap S)\),
  where \(\iota_p : p \to M\) is the canonical injection. Project-local helper consumed
  by the two kernel-dimension computations of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  The inclusion \(\iota_p\) is injective, so it restricts to a \(\kappa\)-linear
  isomorphism from \(\iota_p^{-1} S\) onto its image \(\iota_p(\iota_p^{-1} S) = p \cap S\)
  (image of a comap under an injective map). An isomorphism preserves dimension, giving
  the claimed equality.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
  %   intersection dimension).
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
  \[
    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
  \]
  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
\end{lemma}

**Scope**: Whole-blueprint audit (all 6 chapters).

|---|---|

|---|---|

#### Active route declaration chain (all with statement `\leanok`):
`def:pushforward_base_change_map` → `lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk` → `lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis` → `lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens` → tilde-dictionary chain (`lem:gammaPushforwardIso`, `lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso`, `lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt`, `lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule`, `lem:powers_restrictScalars`, `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`, `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional`, `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`, `lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso`) → base-change-mate seam chain (`lem:base_change_map_affine_local`, `lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor`, `lem:base_change_mate_domain_read`, `lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read`, `lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso`, `lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso`, `lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv`, `lem:base_change_mate_unit_value`, `def:base_change_mate_inner_value`, `lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp`, `lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id`, `lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id`, `lem:gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom`) → **`lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose`** (prover target) → `lem:base_change_mate_section_identity` → `lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace` → `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange` → `lem:affine_base_change_pushforward` → `thm:flat_base_change_pushforward`.

#### Gate-critical: `lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose` — audit
- **Statement** (lines 1986–2047): `\leanok` present. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}`. `\uses{}` block lists: `lem:base_change_mate_domain_read`, `lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read`, `lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv`, `lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso`, `lem:base_change_mate_unit_value`. All confirmed `\leanok`.

  - **Step (i) — counit factorization**: the base-change map factors via `Adjunction.homEquiv_counit` as `Γ(g^*(θ_in)) ≫ Γ(ε_g)`; no opaque transpose remains. Named Mathlib lemma cited.
  - **Step (ii) — concrete counit coherence**: explains WHY `Π_ψ` (the pullback dictionary from `lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso`) cannot be simplified by reflexivity; requires the abstract conjugate calculus. The relevant identity is the counit-triangle zig-zag for the composed adjunction `(g^* ⊣ g_*) ∘ (Γ ⊣ ~)`, the dual companion of `lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib`.

- **leandag**: `lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose` listed as a **leaf** in the DAG — consistent with it being an open proof target that nothing yet depends on from downstream in the same axiom-free path.
